subject: maths
title: Limits and Derivatives
book: NCERT Class 11 maths — Limits and Derivatives

nodes.title: Interpret the derivative as an instantaneous rate and the slope of the tangent
nodes.brief: Build the intuitive idea of a derivative: shrink the averaging interval until the average rate of change becomes the instantaneous rate, equal to the slope of the tangent to the curve.
nodes.quote: the instantaneous velocity v(t) of a body at time t = 2 is equal to the slope of the tangent of the curve
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Forming the average rate of change over an interval.
For a body with position s = 4.9t^2, the average velocity from time t to time t+h is [s(t+h) - s(t)]/h = [4.9(t+h)^2 - 4.9t^2]/h. For t=2 this simplifies to 4.9(4+h), which equals 4.9*5 = 24.5 m/s for h=1, or 4.9*4.5 = 22.05 m/s for h=0.5 - a different number depending on the interval chosen.

PART 2 — Shrinking the interval from both sides until the averages converge.
As h is taken smaller and smaller toward 0 (from either side: h=0.1 gives 4.9*4.1=20.09, h=0.01 gives 4.9*4.01=19.649), the average velocities approach a single value 4.9*4 = 19.6 m/s. This common value is not affected by which side you approach from.

PART 3 — The limiting value is the instantaneous rate and the slope of the tangent.
The number 19.6 m/s is the instantaneous velocity at t=2 - the rate of change of position at that single instant. Geometrically, each average velocity is the slope of a chord from P to Q on the s-t curve; as h shrinks to 0 the chord rotates to become the tangent at P, so the instantaneous rate equals the slope of the tangent line.
keyMoves: Form the average rate of change [s(t2) - s(t1)]/(t2 - t1) over an interval
Shrink the interval from both sides and watch the averages approach one value
Identify that limiting value as the instantaneous rate = slope of the tangent at the point
misconceptions: Treating the average velocity over a finite interval as the velocity at a single instant
Thinking only one side (left or right) of the instant matters when closing in

nodes.title: State the limit of a function as the value it should assume at a point
nodes.brief: Understand a limit intuitively: as x is taken closer and closer to a, f(x) settles toward a value l, written lim x->a f(x) = l - the value f(x) should assume at x = a.
nodes.quote: The limit of f(x) as x tends to zero is to be thought of as the value f(x) should assume at x = 0.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Evaluating f(x) at points close to a, not at a.
To investigate lim x->2 h(x) where h(x) = (x^2-4)/(x-2), note h(2) is undefined (0/0). Instead compute h at x=1.9, 1.99, 1.999 and x=2.1, 2.01, 2.001 - these are points near 2 but not equal to 2.

PART 2 — Reading the value the function clusters toward.
At x=1.9: h = (3.61-4)/(1.9-2) = (-0.39)/(-0.1) = 3.9. At x=1.99: h = (3.9601-4)/(-0.01) = 3.99. As x gets closer to 2 from either side, h(x) clusters toward 4. That single value 4 is the limit: lim x->2 h(x) = 4.

PART 3 — Distinguishing the limit from the function value at the point.
The limit l = 4 is the value h(x) should assume at x=2, as dictated by nearby behaviour. It is NOT the value h(2) - indeed h(2) is undefined here. The limit describes what the function approaches, not necessarily what it equals at the point. A function can have a perfectly good limit at a point where it is undefined or even takes a different value.
keyMoves: Evaluate f(x) at points approaching a (not at a itself)
Read off the single value l those f(x) cluster toward
Distinguish the limit l from the actual value f(a), which may differ or be undefined
misconceptions: Assuming lim x->a f(x) must equal f(a)
Concluding no limit exists merely because f(a) is undefined

nodes.title: Use left- and right-hand limits to decide whether a limit exists
nodes.brief: Compute the left-hand and right-hand limits separately and conclude the (two-sided) limit exists only when they coincide.
nodes.quote: If the right and left hand limits coincide, we call that common value as the limit
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Computing the left-hand limit: x approaching a from below.
The left-hand limit lim x->a^- f(x) uses only values of x slightly less than a. For the step function g(x) = 1 if x<=0 and g(x) = 2 if x>0, approaching x=0 from the left means x=-0.1, -0.01, ... where g(x)=1 throughout, so lim x->0^- g(x) = 1.

PART 2 — Computing the right-hand limit: x approaching a from above.
The right-hand limit lim x->a^+ f(x) uses only values of x slightly greater than a. For the same step function, x=0.1, 0.01, ... all give g(x)=2, so lim x->0^+ g(x) = 2.

PART 3 — The two-sided limit exists only when both one-sided limits coincide.
The ordinary limit lim x->a f(x) exists and equals l if and only if lim x->a^- f(x) = lim x->a^+ f(x) = l. For the step function, LHL=1 != RHL=2, so the limit at x=0 does not exist - even though g(0)=1 is defined. Always compute both one-sided limits before declaring the two-sided limit.
keyMoves: Compute lim x->a^- f(x) using values just left of a
Compute lim x->a^+ f(x) using values just right of a
Declare the limit to exist (= common value) iff the two one-sided limits are equal
misconceptions: Reporting a limit from only one side without checking the other
Assuming the limit exists because f(a) is defined, even when the one-sided limits differ

nodes.title: Apply the algebra of limits to sums, differences, products and quotients
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 1: when the limits of f and g both exist, the limit distributes over sum, difference, product and quotient (denominator non-zero), with a constant pulling out.
nodes.quote: the limiting process respects addition, subtraction, multiplication and division as long as the limits and functions under consideration are well defined
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Distributing the limit over sums, differences, and products.
If lim x->a f(x) and lim x->a g(x) both exist, then lim[f +/- g] = lim f +/- lim g and lim[f*g] = (lim f)(lim g). For example, lim x->2 [x^2 + 3x] = lim x^2 + lim 3x = 4 + 6 = 10.

PART 2 — Using the quotient rule only when the denominator limit is non-zero.
For a ratio, lim[f/g] = (lim f)/(lim g) is valid ONLY when lim g != 0. If lim g = 0, the quotient rule cannot be applied directly (the 0/0 case needs special handling; a non-zero over zero case means the limit does not exist).

PART 3 — Pulling a constant factor outside the limit.
For any constant lambda, lim x->a [lambda * f(x)] = lambda * lim x->a f(x). This and the additive rule let you treat each term of a complicated expression separately, which is the workhorse for evaluating polynomial and rational limits.
keyMoves: Split lim [f +/- g] into lim f +/- lim g and lim [f.g] into (lim f)(lim g)
Use lim [f/g] = (lim f)/(lim g) only when lim g is non-zero
Pull a constant factor lambda outside the limit
misconceptions: Applying the quotient rule when the denominator's limit is zero
Using the algebra rules when one of the individual limits does not exist

nodes.title: Evaluate the limit of a polynomial by substitution
nodes.brief: Use the fact that lim x->a x^n = a^n with the algebra of limits to show a polynomial's limit at a is just its value f(a).
nodes.quote: Hence the limits are the values of the function at the prescribed points
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Using lim x->a x^n = a^n for each power term.
From lim x->a x = a, the product rule of limits gives lim x->a x^2 = a^2, and by induction lim x->a x^n = a^n for any positive integer n. A constant coefficient a_k pulls outside: lim a_k x^k = a_k a^k.

PART 2 — Combining terms with the sum rule.
A polynomial f(x) = a_n x^n + ... + a_1 x + a_0 is a sum of constant-multiple-of-power terms. Applying the sum and constant-multiple rules term by term: lim x->a f(x) = a_n * a^n + ... + a_1 * a + a_0 = f(a).

PART 3 — Conclusion: substitute directly.
The limit of any polynomial at any point equals the polynomial''s value there - just substitute x = a. For example lim x->3 [x(x+1)] = 3*4 = 12, and lim x->1 [x^3-x^2+1] = 1-1+1 = 1. No special manipulation is needed for polynomials.
keyMoves: Use lim x->a x^n = a^n term by term
Combine terms with the sum and constant-multiple limit rules
Conclude lim x->a f(x) = f(a) for any polynomial - just substitute
misconceptions: Believing a polynomial limit needs special manipulation rather than plain substitution
Mishandling negative or zero powers when substituting

nodes.title: Evaluate limits of rational functions, resolving the 0/0 form by cancelling factors
nodes.brief: Evaluate a rational function by substitution; if the result is 0/0, cancel the common (x - a) factors first, and recognise the non-zero-over-zero case where the limit does not exist.
nodes.quote: If this is of the form  $\frac{0}{0}$ , we try to rewrite the function cancelling the factors which are causing the limit to be of the form  $\frac{0}{0}$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitution when the denominator is non-zero.
For a rational function f(x) = g(x)/h(x), first try x = a. If h(a) != 0 the limit is simply g(a)/h(a). For example lim x->2 (x+3)/(x+1) = 5/3 directly.

PART 2 — Handling the 0/0 form by factoring and cancelling.
If both g(a) = 0 and h(a) = 0 you have the indeterminate form 0/0. Factor out (x-a) from both numerator and denominator and cancel it (valid since x != a in a limit). Re-evaluate at x = a. For lim x->1 (x^2-1)/(x-1): factor as (x-1)(x+1)/(x-1) = x+1, so the limit is 1+1 = 2.

PART 3 — Recognising limits that do not exist (non-zero over zero).
If g(a) != 0 but h(a) = 0 there is no common factor to cancel; the values blow up and the limit does not exist. For example lim x->1 1/(x-1) does not exist because the numerator tends to 1 while the denominator tends to 0.
keyMoves: Substitute x = a; if h(a) != 0 the limit is g(a)/h(a)
If 0/0, factor numerator and denominator and cancel the common (x - a) factors
If g(a) != 0 but h(a) = 0, state that the limit does not exist
misconceptions: Declaring the limit non-existent at a 0/0 form instead of cancelling factors
Cancelling a factor without noting it is the cause of the 0/0, or treating non-zero/0 as 0/0

nodes.title: Apply the standard limit (x^n - a^n)/(x - a) = n a^(n-1)
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 2: lim x->a (x^n - a^n)/(x - a) = n a^(n-1) (true for any rational n, a > 0) to evaluate limits of difference-quotient type.
nodes.quote: An easy exercise in induction on *n* tells us that
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognising the standard pattern (x^n - a^n)/(x - a).
When a limit has the form (x^n - a^n)/(x-a), Theorem 2 applies: lim x->a (x^n - a^n)/(x-a) = n * a^(n-1). This follows from the factored form x^n - a^n = (x-a)(x^(n-1) + x^(n-2)a + ... + a^(n-1)), a sum of n terms each tending to a^(n-1) as x->a.

PART 2 — Applying the formula directly, including rational n.
For any n (positive integer, and extended to rational n when a > 0): plug in and multiply. Example: lim x->2 (x^5-32)/(x-2) = 5 * 2^4 = 5 * 16 = 80. The remark in NCERT extends the result to all real n.

PART 3 — Splitting a ratio of two standard limits.
When numerator and denominator are each of the standard form with the same a, divide the formula values: lim x->1 (x^15-1)/(x^10-1) = [15*1^14]/[10*1^9] = 15/10 = 3/2. Write each part separately first, then form the ratio.
keyMoves: Recognise the (x^n - a^n)/(x - a) pattern in a limit
Apply lim = n a^(n-1) directly (including rational n with a > 0)
Split a ratio of two such forms into a quotient of n a^(n-1) values
misconceptions: Writing the answer as a^(n-1) and forgetting the factor n
Using the formula when a is not positive for non-integer n

nodes.title: Use the Sandwich Theorem to evaluate a squeezed limit
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 4: if f(x) <= g(x) <= h(x) near a and f and h share the same limit l at a, then g also has limit l at a.
nodes.quote: come in handy in calculating limits of some trigonometric functions
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Bounding the target function above and below.
The Sandwich (Squeeze) Theorem states: if f(x) <= g(x) <= h(x) for all x in a common domain near a, and lim x->a f(x) = l = lim x->a h(x), then lim x->a g(x) = l as well. The function g is trapped (sandwiched) between f and h and is forced to the same limit.

PART 2 — Checking the bounding functions share the same limit.
The condition is that BOTH the lower bound f and the upper bound h converge to the SAME value l at the point a. If lim f != lim h the theorem cannot be applied. For (sin x)/x: the geometric inequality gives cos x <= (sin x)/x <= 1 for 0 < x < pi/2, and lim x->0 cos x = 1 = lim x->0 1, so both bounds share limit 1.

PART 3 — Concluding the squeezed limit.
Since (sin x)/x is bounded below by cos x and above by 1, and both bounds have limit 1 as x->0, the Sandwich Theorem forces lim x->0 (sin x)/x = 1. This is the fundamental trigonometric limit. The same argument applies symmetrically for x approaching from the negative side, confirming the two-sided limit.
keyMoves: Bound the target g(x) above and below by functions f and h
Check f and h have a common limit l at the point
Conclude g has the same limit l
misconceptions: Concluding the squeeze when the bounding functions have different limits
Forgetting the inequality must hold throughout a neighbourhood of the point

nodes.title: Apply the standard trigonometric limits (sin x)/x -> 1 and (1 - cos x)/x -> 0
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 5: lim x->0 (sin x)/x = 1 and lim x->0 (1 - cos x)/x = 0, with the algebra of limits, to evaluate trigonometric limits.
nodes.quote: **Theorem 5** The following are two important limits.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Using the two fundamental trigonometric limits.
lim x->0 (sin x)/x = 1 (proved via the Sandwich Theorem with cos x <= (sin x)/x <= 1) and lim x->0 (1-cos x)/x = 0 (using 1-cos x = 2 sin^2(x/2)). Both require x in RADIANS; in degrees the value 1 fails entirely.

PART 2 — Rewriting a trig expression into (sin u)/u pieces with correct scaling.
To evaluate lim x->0 (sin 4x)/(4x), note that if u=4x then as x->0, u->0 and (sin 4x)/(4x) = (sin u)/u -> 1. More generally, lim x->0 (sin cx)/x = c for any constant c, because (sin cx)/x = c*(sin cx)/(cx) -> c*1 = c.

PART 3 — Combining pieces with product and quotient limit rules.
For a ratio like (sin 4x)/(sin 2x) write it as [(sin 4x)/(4x)] * [4x/(2x)] * [(2x)/(sin 2x)] = 1 * 2 * 1 = 2. For (tan x)/x: tan x/x = (sin x/x) * (1/cos x) -> 1 * (1/1) = 1. Algebraic combinations of the two standard limits handle most JEE trigonometric limit questions.
keyMoves: Use lim x->0 (sin x)/x = 1 and lim x->0 (1 - cos x)/x = 0
Rewrite a trig ratio into (sin u)/u pieces, scaling the argument as needed
Combine the pieces with the product/quotient limit rules
misconceptions: Applying (sin x)/x -> 1 with x measured in degrees rather than radians
Forgetting to match the argument scaling, e.g. treating (sin 4x)/x as 1 instead of 4

nodes.title: Compute a derivative from the first-principle limit definition
nodes.brief: Apply the definition f'(a) = lim h->0 [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h (and f'(x) = lim h->0 [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h) to find derivatives from first principles.
nodes.quote: This definition of derivative is also called the first principle of derivative.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Writing the difference quotient [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h.
The derivative of f at x is defined as f'(x) = lim h->0 [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h, provided this limit exists. The expression [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h is the difference quotient: numerator is the change in f, denominator h is the change in x. For f(x)=x^2: [(x+h)^2 - x^2]/h = (2xh + h^2)/h.

PART 2 — Simplifying to cancel the factor of h from the denominator.
Algebra in the numerator must produce a factor of h that cancels the denominator: (2xh + h^2)/h = 2x + h (valid for h != 0). After cancellation, the remaining expression is defined even as h->0. This cancellation is what makes the limit tractable.

PART 3 — Taking h -> 0 to obtain f'(x).
Once h is cancelled, take the limit: lim h->0 (2x + h) = 2x. So f'(x) = 2x for f(x)=x^2. For f(x)=sin x at x=0: [sin h]/h -> 1 as h->0, so f'(0)=1. Evaluating at a specific point a gives a number f'(a) = the slope of the tangent there.
keyMoves: Write the difference quotient [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h
Simplify so the factor of h cancels from the denominator
Take the limit h -> 0 to get f'(x) (or f'(a) at a point)
misconceptions: Setting h = 0 before cancelling, producing 0/0
Confusing f'(a) (a number) with f'(x) (the derivative function)

refreshers.trigger: Student cannot expand (x + h)^2 = x^2 + 2xh + h^2, so cannot turn [(x+h)^2 - x^2]/h into (2xh + h^2)/h.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we go on: if you square the whole thing (x + h), what do you get written out term by term?
ladder: start with two pieces added together: how do you multiply (a + b) by (a + b)? remember each piece on the left hits each piece on the right.
do that multiply: (a + b)(a + b) = a*a + a*b + b*a + b*b. the two middle terms a*b and b*a are the same, so they add up - what does that give you?
so (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. it is NOT just a^2 + b^2 - that loses the middle 2ab term.
now put a = x and b = h into that identity. what is (x + h)^2?
refreshers.answer: (a + b)^2 means (a + b)(a + b), and multiplying out (each term times each term) gives a^2 + ab + ba + b^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 - the middle term 2ab is the one people drop. Putting a = x and b = h: (x + h)^2 = x^2 + 2xh + h^2. That is exactly what we need here: the numerator of the difference quotient is (x+h)^2 - x^2 = (x^2 + 2xh + h^2) - x^2 = 2xh + h^2, which is why [(x+h)^2 - x^2]/h becomes (2xh + h^2)/h.
refreshers.returnCue: good - so (x+h)^2 - x^2 = 2xh + h^2. now we put that over h and cancel to get 2x + h, then send h to 0.

nodes.title: Interpret f'(a) geometrically as the slope of the tangent
nodes.brief: Read the first-principle difference quotient as the slope of a chord, and its limit f'(a) as the slope of the tangent to y = f(x) at the point.
nodes.quote: Thus the limit turns out to be equal to the slope of the tangent.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identifying the chord slope as the difference quotient.
Take two points on y = f(x): P = (a, f(a)) and Q = (a+h, f(a+h)). The slope of the chord PQ (the straight line through P and Q) is the rise over the run: [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h. This is exactly the difference quotient from the first-principle definition.

PART 2 — Letting h -> 0 to collapse the chord into the tangent.
As h decreases toward 0, the point Q slides along the curve toward P. The chord PQ rotates and in the limit becomes the tangent line to the curve at P. Therefore, the limit of the chord slopes is the slope of the tangent: lim h->0 [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h = f'(a) = slope of tangent at P.

PART 3 — Reading f'(a) as tan(psi).
If psi is the angle the tangent makes with the positive x-axis, then the slope of the tangent equals tan(psi). So f'(a) = tan(psi). This ties the analytic definition directly to the geometric picture: f'(a) is a number you can read off the graph as the steepness of the curve at the point (a, f(a)).
keyMoves: Identify [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h as the slope of the chord through P and Q
Let h -> 0 so the chord becomes the tangent at P
Read f'(a) as the slope of that tangent, f'(a) = tan(psi)
misconceptions: Confusing the slope of the chord (a finite-h secant) with the tangent slope (the limit)
Thinking the tangent slope depends on the choice of the second point Q

refreshers.trigger: Student reaches f'(a) = tan(psi) but does not recall that the slope of a straight line equals the tangent of the angle it makes with the positive x-axis (slope = tan of inclination).
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we read this as an angle: if a line tilts up at some angle from the x-axis, how is that angle linked to its slope?
ladder: Slope is rise over run: for a line going through (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) it is (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1).

Now picture a little right triangle under the line: the run is the horizontal side, the rise is the vertical side, and the line itself is the hypotenuse. Which side is opposite the angle psi the line makes with the x-axis, and which is adjacent?
In that triangle, tan(psi) = opposite/adjacent = rise/run. So tan(psi) is the same rise-over-run we just called the slope.
So what single equation links the slope of a line to the angle psi it makes with the positive x-axis?
refreshers.answer: For a straight line that makes an angle psi with the positive x-axis (its angle of inclination), the slope equals tan(psi). The reason is the right triangle under the line: tan(psi) = opposite/adjacent = rise/run, and rise/run is exactly the slope. So a steeper line means a larger psi and a larger tan(psi); a flat line has psi = 0 and slope tan(0) = 0; a line at 45 degrees has slope tan(45) = 1. That is why, once we found f'(a) is the slope of the tangent at P, we can read it as an angle: f'(a) = tan(psi), where psi is the angle the tangent makes with the positive x-axis.
refreshers.returnCue: so slope and tan(psi) are the same number - that's why f'(a) = tan(psi) lets us read the derivative straight off the graph as the steepness of the tangent.

nodes.title: Differentiate combinations using the sum, difference, product and quotient rules
nodes.brief: Apply the derivative rules: (u +/- v)' = u' +/- v', the product rule (uv)' = u'v + uv', and the quotient rule (u/v)' = (u'v - uv')/v^2.
nodes.quote: we expect the rules for derivatives to follow closely that of limits
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The sum and difference rules: differentiate termwise.
d/dx[u +/- v] = u' +/- v'. This means you can differentiate a sum or difference by handling each term separately: d/dx[x^3 + sin x] = 3x^2 + cos x. Constant factors also pull through: d/dx[5x^2] = 5 * 2x = 10x.

PART 2 — The product rule (Leibnitz rule): (uv)' = u'v + uv'.
For two differentiable functions u and v: d/dx[u*v] = u'*v + u*v'. It is NOT u'*v' (multiplying derivatives). Example: d/dx[x^2 * (x+1)] = 2x*(x+1) + x^2*1 = 2x^2+2x+x^2 = 3x^2+2x.

PART 3 — The quotient rule: (u/v)' = (u'v - uv')/v^2.
For a ratio (v != 0): d/dx[u/v] = (u'*v - u*v')/v^2. The numerator is u'v MINUS uv' in that order - getting the sign right is critical. Example: d/dx[(x+1)/x] = [1*x - (x+1)*1]/x^2 = (x - x - 1)/x^2 = -1/x^2.
keyMoves: Differentiate sums/differences termwise: (u +/- v)' = u' +/- v'
Apply the product rule (uv)' = u'v + uv'
Apply the quotient rule (u/v)' = (u'v - uv')/v^2 (v != 0)
misconceptions: Writing (uv)' = u'v' (differentiating factors separately) instead of u'v + uv'
Getting the sign or order wrong in the quotient rule numerator (u'v - uv')

nodes.title: Differentiate a power x^n using the power rule n x^(n-1)
nodes.brief: Apply Theorem 6: the derivative of f(x) = x^n is n x^(n-1) (true for any real power n), derived from first principles via the binomial expansion or induction with the product rule.
nodes.quote: **Remark** The above theorem is true for all powers of *x*, i.e., *n* can be any real number (but we will not prove it here).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The power rule: d/dx x^n = n x^(n-1).
For any real number n, differentiating x^n gives n times x to the power (n-1). Examples: d/dx x^3 = 3x^2; d/dx x^5 = 5x^4; d/dx x = 1*x^0 = 1; d/dx (constant) = 0 because a constant is x^0 differentiated with n=0 gives 0.

PART 2 — Extending to negative and fractional powers.
The rule works unchanged for negative integers: d/dx x^(-1) = (-1)x^(-2) = -1/x^2. For fractions: d/dx x^(1/2) = (1/2)x^(-1/2) = 1/(2 sqrt(x)). The exponent always drops by 1 and multiplies out front.

PART 3 — Recovery of special cases.
Setting n=1: d/dx x = 1*x^0 = 1. Setting n=0 (constant c = c*x^0): d/dx c = 0. The single formula d/dx x^n = n x^(n-1) covers every power you will encounter in JEE: polynomials (non-negative integers), reciprocal powers (negative integers), surds (fractions).
keyMoves: Apply d/dx x^n = n x^(n-1) for any real n
Handle negative and fractional powers by the same rule (e.g. d/dx x^(-1) = -x^(-2))
Recover special cases d/dx x = 1 and d/dx (constant) = 0
misconceptions: Forgetting to reduce the exponent by 1 (writing n x^n)
Restricting the rule to positive integers when it holds for all real n

nodes.title: Differentiate a polynomial term by term
nodes.brief: Use Theorem 7: differentiate a_n x^n + ... + a_1 x + a_0 term by term to get n a_n x^(n-1) + (n-1) a_(n-1) x^(n-2) + ... + a_1.
nodes.quote: We start with the following theorem which tells us the derivative of a polynomial function.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiating each power term a_k x^k independently.
By the sum rule and the power rule, each term a_k x^k contributes k*a_k*x^(k-1) to the derivative. Work through every term: d/dx[x^4] = 4x^3, d/dx[-4x^3] = -12x^2, and so on. Do not skip terms.

PART 2 — Dropping the constant and collecting results.
The constant term a_0 differentiates to 0 (d/dx a_0 = 0) and simply vanishes. Sum all the differentiated power terms to get f'(x). For f(x) = 6x^100 - x^55 + x: f'(x) = 600x^99 - 55x^54 + 1.

PART 3 — Evaluating the derivative at a point.
Substitute x = a into f'(x) to get a number f'(a). For f(x) = 1 + x + x^2 + ... + x^50: f'(x) = 1 + 2x + ... + 50x^49, so f'(1) = 1 + 2 + ... + 50 = 50*51/2 = 1275. This numerical value is the slope of the tangent at x=a or the instantaneous rate of change there.
keyMoves: Differentiate each term a_k x^k to k a_k x^(k-1)
Drop the constant term and sum the results
Substitute a value if a numerical derivative f'(a) is wanted
misconceptions: Leaving the constant term in the derivative
Differentiating only the leading term instead of every term

nodes.title: Differentiate trigonometric functions using first principles and the rules
nodes.brief: Establish d/dx(sin x) = cos x and d/dx(tan x) = sec^2 x from first principles, then differentiate other trig functions with the product and quotient rules.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving d/dx(sin x) = cos x and d/dx(cos x) = -sin x from first principles.
Using the addition formula sin(x+h) - sin x = 2 cos(x + h/2) sin(h/2), the difference quotient becomes [2 cos(x+h/2) sin(h/2)]/h = cos(x+h/2) * [sin(h/2)/(h/2)]. As h->0: cos(x+h/2) -> cos x and [sin(h/2)/(h/2)] -> 1 (the fundamental trig limit). Result: d/dx(sin x) = cos x. Similarly d/dx(cos x) = -sin x.

PART 2 — Getting d/dx(tan x) = sec^2 x and d/dx(cot x) = -cosec^2 x.
Apply the quotient rule to tan x = sin x / cos x: [(cos x)(cos x) - (sin x)(-sin x)] / cos^2 x = (cos^2 x + sin^2 x)/cos^2 x = 1/cos^2 x = sec^2 x. For cot x = cos x / sin x: quotient rule gives (-sin^2 x - cos^2 x)/sin^2 x = -1/sin^2 x = -cosec^2 x.

PART 3 — Using product and quotient rules for trig combinations.
With d/dx(sin x)=cos x and d/dx(cos x)=-sin x as building blocks, differentiate any trig combination: d/dx(sin^2 x) = 2 sin x cos x (product rule with u=v=sin x); d/dx(x^2 sin x) = 2x sin x + x^2 cos x (product rule); d/dx[(x-cos x)/sin x] uses the quotient rule. Always check that angles are in radians.
keyMoves: Derive d/dx(sin x) = cos x and d/dx(cos x) = -sin x from first principles + trig limits
Get d/dx(tan x) = sec^2 x and d/dx(cot x) = -cosec^2 x
Differentiate products/quotients of trig functions with the product and quotient rules
misconceptions: Writing d/dx(cos x) = sin x, dropping the minus sign
Applying the trig derivatives with the angle in degrees (they assume radians)

nodes.title: Differentiate polynomial and trigonometric functions by combining limits and derivative rules
nodes.brief: Tie the chapter together: from the limit machinery and the first-principle definition, differentiate any polynomial, rational or trigonometric combination using the power, sum, product and quotient rules.
nodes.quote: The heart of the matter is derivative of a function at a given point in its domain of definition.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Choosing the right method: first principle or standard rules.
If a function is new (not yet in your table of known derivatives), use the first-principle definition f'(x) = lim h->0 [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h. If the function is built from known pieces - polynomials (use power rule), sin x (derivative cos x), cos x (derivative -sin x), tan x (derivative sec^2 x) - use the standard rules directly. Reaching for first principles when the rules already apply wastes time.

PART 2 — Applying the power, sum, product and quotient rules to any combination.
Differentiate sums termwise, use the product rule (uv)'=u'v+uv' for products, and the quotient rule (u/v)'=(u'v-uv')/v^2 for ratios. For d/dx[(x^2+1)/cos x]: u=x^2+1, u'=2x; v=cos x, v'=-sin x; result: [2x cos x + (x^2+1)sin x]/cos^2 x. The power rule handles every polynomial piece.

PART 3 — Evaluating f'(a) at a point to get a numerical rate or tangent slope.
Substitute x=a into f'(x). For f(x)=x^2+sin x: f'(x)=2x+cos x; f'(pi/2)=pi+0=pi (slope of tangent at x=pi/2). Evaluate correctly by substituting the specific value after differentiating the whole expression - never substitute before differentiating.
keyMoves: Choose the method: first principle for a new function, or the standard rules for known pieces
Apply the power, sum, product and quotient rules (and trig derivatives) to a combination
Evaluate f'(a) at a point when a numerical rate or tangent slope is asked
misconceptions: Reaching for first principles when the standard rules already apply (and vice versa)
Combining the rules incorrectly (e.g. product rule as u'v' or wrong quotient-rule sign)
